title: AI Risk Assessment Methodology
description: AI-specific risk assessment methodology incorporating human rights, bias, safety, transparency, accountability, and regulatory compliance dimensions.
scope: All AI systems developed, deployed, or procured by the organization, including third-party AI components and general-purpose AI models.

approach.name: AI Governance Risk Assessment Framework
approach.basis: Adapted from ISO 31000 and NIST AI RMF with AI-specific extensions for EU AI Act compliance and NGAIGE alignment.

phases.name: Context Establishment
phases.description: Define the AI system scope, stakeholders, deployment context, and applicable regulatory requirements.
activities: Identify the AI system purpose, capabilities, and intended deployment context
Map affected stakeholders including vulnerable or marginalized groups
Determine applicable regulations (EU AI Act, PDPA, GDPR, sector-specific laws)
Establish risk assessment boundaries and assumptions

phases.name: Risk Identification
phases.description: Systematically identify AI-specific risks across all risk dimensions.
activities: Review AI risk taxonomy categories for applicability
Assess training data risks (quality, bias, provenance, privacy)
Evaluate model risks (opacity, drift, adversarial vulnerability, hallucination)
Identify deployment context risks (human oversight gaps, automation bias, misuse potential)
Consider societal and environmental risks

phases.name: Risk Analysis
phases.description: Assess likelihood and impact of identified risks using AI-specific scales.
activities: Rate likelihood using the 5-point likelihood scale
Rate impact using the 5-point impact scale with AI-specific harm dimensions
Apply AI-specific risk factors as multipliers or adjustments
Calculate inherent risk score

phases.name: Risk Evaluation
phases.description: Compare risk levels against risk appetite and determine treatment priority.
activities: Map risks to the 5x5 risk matrix
Compare against organizational AI risk appetite thresholds
Prioritize risks for treatment based on severity and regulatory urgency
Identify risks requiring immediate regulatory notification or system suspension

phases.name: Risk Treatment
phases.description: Select and implement appropriate risk treatment strategies.
activities: Select treatment strategy (avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept) per risk
Note: EU AI Act mandates avoidance for prohibited AI practices (Art. 5)
Design control measures and assign implementation owners
Calculate residual risk after treatment
Obtain risk acceptance sign-off for residual risks above threshold

phases.name: Monitoring and Review
phases.description: Continuously monitor risks and review assessment effectiveness.
activities: Implement ongoing risk monitoring with defined KRIs (Key Risk Indicators)
Review risk assessments at defined intervals or triggered by material changes
Update risk register with new findings and control effectiveness data
Report risk status to governance body at defined frequency

likelihoodScale.description: 5-point scale for assessing probability of risk materialization.

levels.label: Rare
levels.description: Extremely unlikely to occur. No known precedent. Less than 5% probability within assessment period.

levels.label: Unlikely
levels.description: Could occur in exceptional circumstances. Limited precedent. 5-25% probability.

levels.label: Possible
levels.description: Could occur at some point. Some precedent exists. 25-50% probability.

levels.label: Likely
levels.description: Will probably occur in most circumstances. Clear precedent. 50-75% probability.

levels.label: Almost Certain
levels.description: Expected to occur in most circumstances. Frequent precedent. Greater than 75% probability.

impactScale.description: 5-point scale for assessing severity of risk impact, with AI-specific harm dimensions.

levels.label: Negligible
levels.description: Minimal impact. Easily remediated with no lasting effect.

dimensions.humanRights: No discernible effect on individual rights or dignity
dimensions.discriminationBias: No measurable differential impact across groups
dimensions.transparency: Minor documentation gap with no stakeholder impact
dimensions.regulatory: No regulatory concern. No financial penalty exposure.

levels.label: Minor
levels.description: Limited impact affecting small number of individuals. Remediation straightforward.

dimensions.humanRights: Temporary inconvenience to individuals, quickly remedied
dimensions.discriminationBias: Minor statistical disparity below materiality threshold
dimensions.safety: Minor discomfort or inconvenience, no lasting harm
dimensions.accountability: Responsibility gaps identified but no harm resulted
dimensions.regulatory: Informal regulatory inquiry. Potential warning. Fines under 0.5% turnover.

levels.label: Moderate
levels.description: Significant impact affecting identifiable groups. Remediation requires effort and resources.

dimensions.humanRights: Measurable restriction of individual rights requiring remediation
dimensions.discriminationBias: Statistically significant disparity affecting identifiable demographic group
dimensions.safety: Recoverable harm requiring medical attention or support services
dimensions.transparency: Material lack of disclosure leading to uninformed decisions
dimensions.accountability: Unclear accountability contributing to delayed incident response
dimensions.regulatory: Formal regulatory investigation. Corrective action required. Fines up to 1.5% turnover.

levels.label: Major
levels.description: Severe impact affecting large groups. Significant resources required for remediation.

dimensions.humanRights: Serious violation of fundamental rights affecting vulnerable populations
dimensions.discriminationBias: Systematic discrimination affecting protected group with material consequences
dimensions.safety: Serious physical or psychological harm requiring significant intervention
dimensions.transparency: Complete opacity in high-stakes decisions affecting many individuals
dimensions.accountability: No accountable party identified for significant AI-caused harm
dimensions.regulatory: Enforcement action with sanctions. Market restrictions. Fines up to 3% global turnover (EU AI Act Art. 99).

levels.label: Critical
levels.description: Catastrophic impact. Irreversible harm. Existential threat to organization or severe societal harm.

dimensions.humanRights: Widespread, irreversible violation of fundamental rights and human dignity
dimensions.discriminationBias: Systemic discrimination at scale causing irreversible harm to protected communities
dimensions.safety: Loss of life, permanent disability, or widespread physical harm
dimensions.transparency: Complete deception or manipulation undermining democratic processes
dimensions.accountability: Total accountability failure enabling ongoing, unchecked harm
dimensions.regulatory: Criminal prosecution. Operating ban. Maximum fines up to 7% global turnover or EUR 35 million (EU AI Act Art. 99). License revocation.

aiSpecificRiskFactors.description: Additional factors that modify risk assessment for AI systems. These factors should be considered as qualitative adjustments to the base likelihood and impact scores.

factors.name: Training Data Quality
factors.description: Quality, representativeness, and provenance of data used to train the AI model.
highRiskIndicators: Training data provenance unknown or poorly documented
Known demographic gaps or underrepresentation in training data
Training data contains personal data without clear legal basis
Data quality checks not automated or performed inconsistently
lowRiskIndicators: Comprehensive data lineage and provenance documentation
Automated quality checks with defined thresholds
Representative sampling validated across demographics
Clear legal basis documented for all data sources

factors.name: Model Explainability
factors.description: Degree to which AI model decisions can be understood and explained to affected individuals.
highRiskIndicators: Black-box model with no explainability methods implemented
Model used for high-stakes decisions (credit, employment, justice) without explanations
Explanations technically available but not comprehensible to affected individuals
No mechanism for individuals to contest AI-driven decisions
lowRiskIndicators: Inherently interpretable model or validated post-hoc explainability methods
Explanations tested with representative end users for comprehensibility
Both global and local explanations available
Contestation mechanism with human review established

factors.name: Deployment Context
factors.description: The environment and circumstances in which the AI system operates.
highRiskIndicators: AI system affects fundamental rights (employment, credit, justice, healthcare)
Deployed in contexts involving vulnerable populations (children, elderly, disabled)
Operates in safety-critical environment (autonomous vehicles, medical devices)
Cross-border deployment across multiple regulatory jurisdictions
lowRiskIndicators: Low-stakes decisions with easy human override
Used as decision support rather than autonomous decision-making
Deployed in controlled environment with trained operators
Single jurisdiction with clear regulatory framework

factors.name: Human Oversight Level
factors.description: The degree and effectiveness of human oversight over AI system decisions.
highRiskIndicators: Fully autonomous decision-making with no human review
Human oversight is nominal (rubber-stamp approval of AI recommendations)
Operators not trained on AI system limitations and failure modes
No kill switch or emergency override mechanism
lowRiskIndicators: Human-in-the-loop for all consequential decisions
Operators trained on limitations with documented competency
Kill switch tested periodically with documented results
Override patterns analyzed to detect automation bias

factors.name: Model Maturity and Stability
factors.description: The maturity of the AI model and its stability in production.
highRiskIndicators: Newly deployed model without production track record
Frequent model updates without formal validation gates
Known model drift without retraining procedures
Generative AI with known hallucination tendencies in deployment domain
lowRiskIndicators: Established model with stable production performance over extended period
Formal model lifecycle management with validated update procedures
Continuous drift monitoring with automated alerts
Known limitations documented and mitigated in deployment

factors.name: Scale and Reach
factors.description: The number of individuals and decisions affected by the AI system.
highRiskIndicators: AI system affects millions of individuals
Decisions are difficult or impossible to reverse
Cumulative impact across multiple AI systems affecting same population
System output feeds into other automated decision systems
lowRiskIndicators: Limited number of affected individuals
Decisions easily reversible
Standalone system with no downstream automated dependencies
Impact limited to operational efficiency without individual consequences

riskAcceptanceCriteria.description: Criteria for determining acceptable residual risk levels.

critical.action: Immediate escalation to board. System suspension until risk reduced. Regulatory notification may be required.

high.action: Senior management approval required. Time-bound treatment plan mandatory. Enhanced monitoring.

medium.action: Risk owner approval required. Treatment plan with defined timeline. Standard monitoring.

low.action: Accept with documentation. Standard review cycle.
